title: Hanging Outdoor Bed
summary: A porch-swing-style daybed on a doubled 2x6/2x4 support frame, hung from the ceiling by chain and eye bolts sized to carry a twin mattress and sleeper.
description:
The load path on this bed is the whole design: weight goes from the mattress, to the slats, to the cleats, to the side 2x6s, and only then to the eye bolts — which is exactly why the eye bolts get installed on the longer 2x6 sides and nowhere else. Doubling up the 2x6 frame with a 2x4 cleat underneath isn't decorative bulk, it's what gives that final link in the chain — the eye bolt itself — enough solid wood to bite into.

The end and side trim is genuinely optional structurally, and the plan is upfront about that: the frame alone is strong enough without it. It's there purely to dress up the outside of an otherwise stud-grade frame, which is also why it's fine to upgrade to a nicer species for the trim alone while keeping cheap lumber everywhere it won't show. Whatever hanging hardware you choose has to match your actual ceiling structure — the bolt type is a decision made at the rafters, not at this bed.

tools.note: Predrills for the eye bolts and hanging hardware.

materials.name: Eye bolts, exterior-rated
materials.note: Four for the bed, four for the ceiling — sized to match your actual rafters.

materials.name: Chain, exterior-rated, ~24 ft total
materials.note: 24 ft covers a standard 8 ft ceiling; adjust length to your own ceiling height.

materials.name: Twin mattress
materials.note: Not included in the cost estimate — priced separately based on what you already own or choose to buy.

cutList.note: These carry the eye bolts and the full load of the bed.

steps.title: Build the support frame
steps.body: Build the support frame from the frame sides and ends with 2-1/2" screws and glue — three screws per corner. Check for square.

steps.title: Attach the cleats
steps.body: Attach the cleat supports 3/4" from the top edge of the frame sides with 2-1/2" screws and glue — doubling up the 2x6 and 2x4 this way creates a genuinely strong support system for the slats. Also screw from the outside of the frame ends into the edges of the cleats.

steps.title: Add the end trim
steps.body: Attach the end trim with nails and glue, flush at the bottom and ends, letting the top edge form a small lip that keeps the mattress from sliding off the platform. This trim is purely cosmetic — the frame is plenty strong without it.

steps.title: Add the side trim
steps.body: Add the side trim the same way as the end trim.

steps.title: Add the slats and finish
steps.body: Screw the slats down across the cleats. Sand every surface and apply an exterior finish.

steps.title: Install the eye bolts
steps.body: Predrill and install the eye bolts in the four corners of the bed frame. It's important these attach into the longer 2x6 sides only, since the load path runs from the slats to the cleats to the sides to the eye bolts.

steps.title: Hang the bed
steps.body: Rest the bed on sawhorses or a table under where you want to hang it. Install matching eye bolts into ceiling rafters — choose bolt hardware based on your actual rafter construction — and hang the bed with chain from all four corners.

images.alt: Hanging outdoor daybed suspended by chains from a porch ceiling
